Policy Name/Title: Scaling Artificial Intelligence in Health: AI in Health Policy Checklist
Issuing Body: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
Summary: Health-specific AI policy checklist developed by the OECD with the Global Digital Health Partnership, Coalition for Health AI, and OECD AI in Health Expert Group. The framework identifies policy actions for responsibly scaling AI in health across four pillars: establishing enablers, building capacity and capability, implementing guardrails, engaging meaningfully with stakeholders, and deploying trustworthy AI. It is non-prescriptive but intended to help policymakers, technologists, and health workforces identify blind spots and readiness gaps.
Healthcare Implications: Provides an international reference framework for governments and health systems developing AI-in-health strategies, action plans, oversight bodies, procurement expectations, data foundations, workforce capacity, public/provider engagement, and trustworthy deployment practices. It can help benchmark national health-AI readiness and guide policy design around health data access, validation, governance, monitoring, transparency, accountability, equity, and cross-border compatibility.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Policy Name/Title: Zimbabwe National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
Summary: Establishes a national framework for the development, deployment, and governance of artificial intelligence across sectors, including healthcare, with emphasis on innovation, capacity-building, and responsible use.
Healthcare Implications: Identifies healthcare as a priority domain for AI adoption, including applications in diagnostics, public health, and system efficiency. While non-binding, it signals national direction that may shape health system investment, governance, and AI deployment practices.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: AI for Care: The Artificial Intelligence Strategy for Healthcare in Ireland 2026-2030
Summary: Ireland’s first national AI strategy for healthcare, setting a 2026-2030 roadmap for the safe, responsible, and effective adoption of artificial intelligence across health and social care, with emphasis on governance, public trust, workforce readiness, and system-wide implementation.
Healthcare Implications: The strategy gives Irish health and social care actors a national framework for evaluating, adopting, and scaling AI in clinical and operational settings, signaling expectations around oversight, safety, ethics, and implementation capacity that can shape procurement, deployment, and future regulatory coordination.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Guidance on Using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Patient Care
Summary: Professional guidance for doctors using AI in patient care. The guidance covers AI embedded in medical devices, AI scribes, AI tools that manage clinical inboxes, and AI systems that influence clinical decisions. It states that doctors remain responsible for clinical decisions and actions, must understand AI capabilities and limitations, check AI outputs where reasonably practicable, document AI use when it influences clinical decision-making, avoid using AI to impersonate themselves, inform patients when AI is used in care, obtain informed consent in specified higher-risk uses, and ensure privacy, data security, safety, and fairness.
Healthcare Implications: Creates a practical professional-responsibility framework for AI use by New Zealand doctors. It directly affects clinical deployment of AI scribes, decision-support tools, inbox-management systems, and AI-enabled devices by emphasizing clinician accountability, patient transparency, consent, documentation, privacy safeguards, data security, suitability for the patient population, and avoidance of bias or worsening inequities. It is guidance rather than legislation, but it gives clear expectations for safe and ethical AI use in patient-facing medical practice.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Title: CFM Resolution No. 2.454/2026 – Use of Artificial Intelligence in Medicine
Issuing Body: Federal Council of Medicine (Conselho Federal de Medicina)
Summary: Regulates the use of artificial intelligence in medical practice in Brazil. The resolution treats AI as a support tool for medical practice, clinical decision-making, health management, research, and education; requires physicians to remain finally responsible for clinical, diagnostic, therapeutic, and prognostic decisions; requires patient disclosure when AI is used as relevant support in care, diagnosis, or treatment; requires preliminary risk classification, governance processes, human supervision, data protection, monitoring, and bias-mitigation measures for medical AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Creates one of the clearest physician-facing AI governance frameworks in the international module. Brazilian physicians and medical institutions using or developing AI must preserve physician autonomy and final medical judgment, explain AI use to patients, document AI use in medical records when used for decision support, avoid delegating diagnosis or therapeutic communication to AI without human mediation, classify AI systems by risk, and implement internal governance and oversight for safety, quality, ethics, privacy, security, and bias mitigation.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Strategy for Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare for India
Issuing Body: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
Summary: National health-AI strategy launched at the India AI Impact Summit 2026 that frames a people-centric roadmap for responsible AI adoption across the health system, including governance expectations, evidence generation, and readiness pillars to support safe, ethical, and trustworthy use.
Healthcare Implications: Sets a national reference point for how Indian health actors evaluate and scale clinical and public-health AI, signaling expectations around governance, validation, data stewardship, and monitoring that can shape procurement, implementation playbooks, and regulator-facing documentation over time.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Framework Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust
Issuing Body: South Korean Ministry of Government Legislation
Summary: South Korea’s comprehensive national AI law, enacted in 2025 and enforced beginning January 22, 2026. The Act defines high-impact AI to include AI used in systems for providing and using healthcare services and AI used in the development and use of medical devices or digital medical devices. It establishes national AI governance structures, transparency duties for high-impact and generative AI, safety and risk-management expectations, confirmation procedures for high-impact AI classification, user protection measures, human management and supervision, documentation duties, and impact-assessment encouragement.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare and medical-device AI are expressly included within the Act’s high-impact AI framework, making the law relevant to Korean health systems, medical AI developers, and digital medical device companies. Operators of healthcare AI may need to assess whether systems qualify as high-impact AI, notify users that products or services are AI-based, maintain risk-management and user-protection measures, support explainability where technically feasible, retain safety/reliability documentation, and preserve human management and supervision. Certified digital medical products may also interact with the Act’s deemed-compliance/safe-harbor structure.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: Strategy on Artificial Intelligence for the Spanish National Health System (eIASNS)
Issuing Body: Ministry of Health/Interterritorial Council of the National Health System
Summary: In November 2025 Spain’s Interterritorial Council of the National Health System approved the Strategy on Artificial Intelligence for the National Health System (eIASNS), a roadmap to coordinate AI use in healthcare based on principles of reliability, usefulness, humanisation and universality, with shared procedures to evaluate, classify and deploy AI tools across regions.
Healthcare Implications: The strategy pushes Spanish health services to adopt validated AI solutions (e.g., imaging diagnosis, virtual assistants, hospital operations) under common governance, ethics and data-protection requirements, accelerating deployment while trying to reduce regional inequities and ensure clinical oversight.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Title: Recommendation on the Ethics of Neurotechnology
Summary: UNESCO’s first global standard-setting instrument on the ethics of neurotechnology, setting principles for governing brain–computer interfaces, neural data, and neurostimulation in line with human rights.
Healthcare Implications: Guides countries to treat neural data as highly sensitive health data, regulate clinical and consumer neurotechnology (including AI-driven brain–computer interfaces), and protect mental privacy, autonomy, and equity in neurotechnology-based care and research.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of AI for Health – Guidance on Large Multi‑Modal Models
Summary: World Health Organization guidance focusing on LMMs in health use cases, covering safety, effectiveness, privacy, bias, accountability, and workforce impacts. Recommends governance measures, evaluation, and monitoring tailored to generative AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals piloting GenAI (e.g., documentation assistants, patient‑facing chat) should align with LMM‑specific safeguards, including transparent labeling, guardrails, and post‑deployment monitoring.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Information for Practitioners Who Provide Virtual Care
Issuing Body: Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency & National Boards
Summary: Updated guidance clarifying that prescribing requires a real‑time consultation (face‑to‑face, video or phone). Asynchronous chats, forms or emails are not a consultation. Flags specific risks when using AI or chatbots and sets expectations for safe virtual care.
Healthcare Implications: Clinicians and telehealth vendors should avoid ‘tick‑box’ prescribing and ensure patient‑clinician interaction before prescribing. Update triage/bot flows, consent, and documentation to reflect that AI/chat is not a consultation for prescribing.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Action Plan for the Use of AI in Operations at the PMDA
Summary: Japan's Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency sets a phased plan to use AI in its internal operations. The plan adopts existing AI tools for search, summarization, minute‑taking, and translation, and pilots proprietary models in a secure environment for specialized review and safety tasks. It also establishes agency‑wide AI governance.
Healthcare Implications: Regulatory reviews, safety assessments, and inspections in Japan may become faster and more consistent as PMDA verifies these tools. Sponsors should anticipate AI‑assisted workflows during interactions and plan submissions with clearer documentation, traceability, and post‑market evidence.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Title: Law No. 132 of 23 Sept 2025 – Provisions and Delegations on Artificial Intelligence
Issuing Body: Parliament of Italy & Presidency of the Republic
Summary: First comprehensive Italian AI law. Sets national AI governance, transparency/labeling duties, enforcement framework; introduces measures addressing deepfakes and sectoral guidance mandates.
Healthcare Implications: Cross-sector law that will govern AI used by public hospitals and health providers (procurement, transparency, risk controls) and AI developers supplying health tools in Italy.

Healthcare Implications: Hospitals can adopt the guidance for governance charters, procurement criteria, pre‑deployment validation gates, and post‑deployment monitoring; vendors align documentation and change‑control to support evaluation and safe clinical use.

Policy Name/Title: Artificial Intelligence Explained – Medical devices
Summary: The Therapeutic Goods Administration's overview of how AI and software are regulated as medical devices, including when AI is considered a device, evidence expectations, and current initiatives on GenAI/LMMs.
Healthcare Implications: Sets expectations for sponsors regarding classification, evidence, and compliance in Australia. Hospitals can use this to understand which AI tools fall under the Therapeutic Goods Administration regulation and what assurances to expect.

Policy Name/Title: Regulation of Software, Including AI – SaMD Rules and Guidance
Summary: Detailed guidance on how the the Therapeutic Goods Administration regulates software-based medical devices, including SaMD definitions, classification, evidence, and obligations across the lifecycle. Recently updated to reflect AI considerations.
Healthcare Implications: Helps developers and providers determine when AI features trigger device regulation and what documentation is required. Supports procurement due diligence and clinical governance for AI-enabled software.

Summary: Technical brief mapping current and emerging uses of artificial intelligence in traditional medicine, cataloguing use cases across diagnosis, treatment, research and health systems, and outlining related opportunities, risks and governance needs.
Healthcare Implications: Highlights how AI could augment traditional medicine services, especially in low- and middle-income settings, while warning of safety, quality, equity and data-governance risks; offers principles and examples to guide health systems and regulators.

Issuing Body: French Ministry of Labour, Health, Solidarity and Families – Digital Health Delegation and partners
Summary: National strategy linking artificial intelligence and health data, setting a 2025–2028 roadmap for governance, infrastructure, and safeguards for secondary use of health data and AI in the French health system in alignment with the European Health Data Space.
Healthcare Implications: Defines how health data will be organised, shared, and used to train and deploy AI in France, reinforcing roles for the Health Data Hub and health data access bodies and strengthening transparency, security, and citizens' rights over secondary use of health data, which will shape development and evaluation of health AI tools.

Healthcare Implications: It provides concrete recommendations for governments, regulators, health systems and developers on how to evaluate, procure and use LMM-based tools in clinical care, public health and research while managing safety, bias, privacy and accountability risks.

Healthcare Implications: Hospitals and vendors face new Electronic Health Record/data duties; researchers and AI developers can request cross‑border datasets for training/validation, analyzed in secure environments under permits, fees, and re‑identification safeguards.

Summary: Government-commissioned review of how existing Australian health, consumer, and device laws apply to AI in health care, identifying gaps and recommending reforms to support safe and responsible AI use in clinical and care settings.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a detailed blueprint for future regulation of AI-enabled clinical decision support, digital health tools, and use of health data in Australia, signalling likely requirements for clearer safety standards, transparency, human oversight, and accountability for health services and vendors deploying AI.

Summary: Final guidance defining machine learning-enabled medical device scope and expectations for evidence, change management (including predetermined change control plans), and lifecycle oversight in pre‑market submissions. Aligns terminology to IMDRF N67 and encourages robust performance evaluation and real‑world monitoring.

Policy Name/Title: Digital Mental Health Technology: Qualification and Classification
Summary: Clarifies when digital mental health technologies (incl. AI chatbots/decision support) qualify as medical devices and how they are classified and evaluated.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects AI mental-health apps used in care pathways: determines device status, evidence expectations and regulatory route before deployment in the NHS.

Policy Name/Title: Pan-Canadian AI for Health (AI4H) Guiding Principles
Summary: Federal, Provincial, and Territorial Health Ministers endorsed shared guiding principles supporting responsible and ethical adoption of AI across Canada’s health systems.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a national governance baseline for safe, equitable, privacy-protective, and transparent use of AI in health and will shape downstream regulation, procurement, and data practices across Canadian health systems.

Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Title: Antiqua et nova. Note on the Relationship Between Artificial Intelligence and Human Intelligence
Issuing Body: Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith and Dicastery for Culture and Education
Summary: Doctrinal Note that articulates Catholic teaching on artificial intelligence, distinguishes human from artificial intelligence, and sets ethical principles for AI development and use across education, the economy, labour, health, human relationships, and warfare.
Healthcare Implications: Emphasizes AI’s potential to enhance healthcare while warning that AI must not replace the doctor–patient relationship and that poorly governed deployment could widen disparities in access to care and reinforce a ‘medicine for the rich’ model.

Policy Name/Title: Five-Year Roadmap to Advance AI in Healthcare (2024–2028)
Policy Type: National AI in health R&D roadmap
Summary: South Korea’s Ministry of Health and Welfare announced a five-year roadmap through 2028 to expand AI R&D in essential healthcare, serious diseases, emergency care, cancer and new drug development, while strengthening medical data platforms and ethical guidelines for AI in medicine.
Healthcare Implications: The roadmap aims to double commercialisation of medical AI technologies, improve access to pseudonymised health data for research and embed AI in clinical trials and hospital workflows, positioning Korea’s health system as an early adopter of AI-enabled care and drug development.

Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Guiding Principles for Conducting Clinical Trial for Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices
Summary: Joint Health Sciences Authority-Ministry of Food and Drug Safety principles for designing and conducting clinical trials of ML‑enabled medical devices. Covers study design, dataset considerations, performance endpoints, drift/updates during trials, and transparency of algorithms.
Healthcare Implications: Provides practical expectations for sponsors and investigators running machine learning-enabled medical device trials in Asia. Hospitals participating in such trials can align protocols, monitoring, and change‑management to the guidance.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Title: NABIDH Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Policy
Summary: DHA’s NABIDH Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Policy sets regulatory and ethical requirements for any AI solution that uses data from Dubai’s NABIDH health information exchange, defining roles for developers, healthcare providers and the authority and requiring compliance with privacy, safety and technical standards.
Healthcare Implications: By tying AI use to NABIDH data-governance controls and patient privacy intelligence tools, the policy enables wider deployment of AI decision-support and analytics in Dubai’s hospitals while mandating oversight, auditability and protection against unauthorised access to patient records.
Keyword Tags: Privacy & Data; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Title: Reflection paper – Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Medicinal Product Lifecycle
Summary: European Medicines Agency reflection paper setting expectations for use of AI/ML across the medicines lifecycle – R&D, clinical trials, manufacturing, and post‑authorisation. Addresses governance, data quality, validation, GxP alignment, and human oversight; provides considerations for submissions and inspections.
Healthcare Implications: Sponsors using AI in trial design, data analysis, pharmacovigilance, or manufacturing should plan transparent methods, validation packages, and risk management. Clinicians participating in trials may see AI‑enabled tools subject to additional documentation and controls.

Policy Name/Title: Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (CETS No. 225)
Summary: First legally binding international treaty on AI addressing human rights, democracy, and rule of law. Sets baseline obligations for parties to ensure AI lifecycle activities respect fundamental rights and provide effective safeguards and remedies.
Healthcare Implications: Health authorities and providers in signatory states should expect cross‑cutting obligations around rights impact assessment, transparency, and accountability in clinical AI. National implementing laws and guidance may flow from the Convention and apply to healthcare delivery and research.

Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public

Policy Name/Title: National Digital Health Strategy 2024–2028
Summary: The Dominican Republic’s Ministry of Health launched the National Digital Health Strategy 2024–2028 to modernise the health system using digital technologies, including interoperable records, telehealth and emerging tools, with support from UNDP, the World Bank and regional partners.
Healthcare Implications: The strategy provides a framework for investment and governance of digital and AI-enabled tools in public health and clinical care, aiming to improve access, efficiency and equity in service delivery while building national capacity for data-driven decision-making.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Artificial Intelligence in Public Health: Readiness Assessment Toolkit (Version 2.0)
Issuing Body: Pan American Health Organization; World Health Organization; Inter-American Development Bank
Summary: Nonbinding readiness assessment toolkit jointly developed by PAHO, WHO, and the Inter-American Development Bank to help countries evaluate their preparedness to integrate artificial intelligence into public health systems. It provides a structured questionnaire and domains covering governance, legal and regulatory frameworks, infrastructure, data management, workforce, funding, implementation, and evaluation.
Healthcare Implications: Supports ministries of health and public health agencies in systematically identifying gaps and prioritizing investments before deploying AI at scale in surveillance, health information systems, and population health programs, helping reduce safety, equity, and privacy risks while promoting more trustworthy and effective use of AI in public health.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 – Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)
Summary: Horizontal EU law establishing risk-based obligations, bans for certain practices, requirements for high‑risk AI (e.g., quality management, data governance, human oversight), transparency duties for specific systems, and market surveillance/enforcement mechanisms.
Healthcare Implications: Many clinical AI products will fall under existing Medical Device Recognition/In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation as medical devices and also under the AI Act as high‑risk AI (Annex II/III cross‑references), driving additional documentation, post‑market monitoring, and transparency. Hospitals and vendors should align conformity assessment, data governance, and human‑oversight controls across MDR/IVDR and the AI Act.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Software and AI as a Medical Device Change Programme – Roadmap
Issuing Body: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
Summary: Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency multi‑work‑package program to update regulation of software and AI as medical devices across the lifecycle, including qualification, pre‑market evidence, post‑market monitoring, and transparency.
Healthcare Implications: Signals evolving UK expectations for AI medical devices. Developers and National Health Service providers should track deliverables that affect submissions, labeling, and post‑market surveillance.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Impact of AI on the Regulation of Medical Products – Policy Paper
Summary: Sets out Medicines & Healthcare products Regulatory Agency's approach to medicines/devices regulation in the age of AI, including evidence expectations, adaptivity, international recognition, and collaboration with the International Medical Device Regulators Forum and other regulators.
Healthcare Implications: Informs sponsors and National Health Service partners about the regulator’s priorities, including transparency and post‑market monitoring for AI‑enabled products. Useful for aligning development plans and regulatory strategies.

Policy Name/Title: Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (v1.0)
Summary: Singapore’s model governance framework tailored to Generative AI, adding dimensions like content provenance, model evaluation/testing, incident reporting, and accountability while building on the 2019/2020 framework. Includes suggested practices and implementation levers.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for hospitals and vendors deploying GenAI for documentation, triage, and patient engagement. Provides concrete controls (e.g., testing, red‑teaming, transparency) that complement clinical safety and privacy requirements.

Policy Name/Title: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: Nonbinding resource describing regulatory considerations for AI in health across the product lifecycle. Addresses data quality, risk management, transparency, post‑market monitoring, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Manufacturers and regulators can use this as a checklist when designing oversight for AI‑enabled health technologies. Hospitals should anticipate documentation needs for procurement and clinical governance.

Policy Name/Title: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative AI Services
Summary: The Cyberspace Administration of China‑led measures regulating public‑facing generative AI services, emphasizing content governance, security assessments, data quality, and user rights. Requires providers to take responsibility for generated content and to implement risk controls.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts China‑based health apps and GenAI tools used by patients/clinicians, imposing content and data controls. International vendors operating in China must adapt service configurations and compliance processes.

Summary: National ethical guidelines establishing a comprehensive framework for responsible development and deployment of AI in biomedical research and healthcare.
Healthcare Implications: Sets expectations for ethics review, data protection, accountability, bias mitigation, consent, and governance of AI tools, guiding clinicians, developers, institutions, and ethics committees across India’s health sector.

Policy Name/Title: Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Global ethical standard adopted by 193 Member States covering human rights, inclusion, data governance, education, and environmental sustainability in AI. Encourages national implementation via action plans and monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems can use the Recommendation to shape equitable AI policies, informed consent and transparency practices, and workforce training. Reinforces safeguards for vulnerable populations in clinical AI deployment.

Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health – WHO Guidance
Summary: World Health Organization guidance outlining ethical challenges and six consensus principles for AI in health, with recommendations for governance, regulation, and rights‑preserving design.
Healthcare Implications: Useful baseline for hospital AI committees and ministries to establish ethics reviews, bias assessment, and transparency practices for AI used in care and public health.

Policy Name/Title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
Summary: Government charter committing agencies to transparency, accountability, and oversight in the use of algorithms, including risk assessment and public communication.
Healthcare Implications: Public health agencies in New Zealand using AI/algorithms should meet Charter commitments – useful reference for transparency and engagement with Māori data sovereignty considerations.

Policy Name/Title: OECD Recommendation on Artificial Intelligence
Summary: First intergovernmental standard on AI adopted by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Council. Sets principles for trustworthy, human‑centred AI and policy recommendations for national strategies, research, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a governance benchmark referenced by many health‑sector policies. Health ministries and systems can map internal AI programs to the Recommendation’s principles to support accountability and cross‑border alignment.
